turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion for the court in case 15-9260, Dean versus United States. Petitioner Levon Dean, Jr. and his brother committed two robberies of drug dealers. During each robbery, the brothers used a gun. Dean was apprehended, tried and convicted of two counts of robbery. He was also convicted of two counts of violating §924(c) of the criminal code. That provision makes it a crime to use or carry a firearm during a violent or drug trafficking crime which Dean and his brother had done during the robberies. Importantly here, §924(c) imposes a distinct penalty. A first conviction under §924(c) carries a five-year mandatory minimum penalty while a second conviction carries an additional 25-year mandatory minimum. These sentences must be imposed “in addition to the punishment provided” for the underlying crimes, that is the robberies themselves in this case. §924(c) also says that any sentence mandated by that provision must run consecutively to any other term of imprisonment imposed on the person including here any sentence for the underlying robberies. For Dean, all these meant that he faced a mandatory 30 years in prison to be served after and in addition to any sentence he received for the robberies. Now at sentencing, Dean urged the district court to take his lengthy mandatory minimum sentences into account when calculating the sentences for the robberies and impose concurrent one day sentences for those counts. Thirty years, Dean argued was more than enough for his crimes and there was no need to add more than a day for the robberies themselves. Now, the judge said he would have agreed to Dean's request, but he understood §924(c) to forbid him from lowering the sentence for the underlying crimes because of the mandatory minimums for the use of a gun in those crimes. On appeal, Dean argued that the district court was wrong to conclude that it could not vary from the regular sentencing range because of the mandatory minimum sentences he would receive. The Court of Appeals however rejected that argument and we decided to review Dean's case. So the question before us is whether in calculating the sentence for an underlying offense, a judge must ignore the fact that the defendant will also serve the mandatory minimum sentences imposed under §924(c). We hold today that the answer is no. A sentencing judge may take those mandatory minimums into account. Sentencing courts have long enjoyed discretion in the information they may consider when setting an appropriate sentence. The sentencing statute lists the factors courts must consider when imposing a sentence. They include “the nature and circumstances of the offense and the history and characteristics of the defendant, as well as the need for the sentence imposed to serve the four overarching aims of sentencing; just punishment, deterrence, protection of the public, and rehabilitation.” Preceding that list of factors is what's known as the parsimony principle, a board command for courts to “impose a sentence sufficient, but not greater than necessary to comply with those four identified purposes of sentencing.” Now these sentencing factors are used to set both the length of separate prison terms and an aggregate prison term comprising separate sentences for multiple counts of conviction. As a general matter, these sentencing provisions allow a Court imposing a sentence on one count of conviction to consider sentences imposed on other counts. But not, the government argues when it comes to the mandatory minimum sentences required under §924(c). The government points out that §924(c), says that the mandatory minimums must be imposed in addition to the punishment provided for the underlying crime, but that says nothing about the length of a sentence on the underlying crime, much less about what information a Court may consider in determining that sentence. If the sentencing judge imposes a sentence of one day for the robberies, that would be consistent with §924(c) so long as the one day was added to the 30 years. The same is true when it comes to the requirement of consecutive sentences in §924(c). Nothing about that requirement prevents a district court from imposing a 30-year mandatory minimum sentence under §924(c) and a one day sentence for the underlying crime provided those terms run one after the other. The government argues that it's clear what Congress meant to do. It wanted the extra sentence for using a gun in connection with the violent crime to be on top of whatever the normal sentence was for the violent crime itself. But Congress did not say that and when it comes to time in prison, we think it is fair to insist that Congress speak clearly. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is reversed and the case remanded for further proceedings. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
